affected_groups: urban flagship schools and their families
content.decision_question: Is it acceptable to let demographic forces alone reshape the selective track's footprint, accepting uneven and unaddressed equity consequences, rather than actively steering the outcome?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can document and even project the geographic unevenness of passive contraction with reasonable confidence given cohort and class-size data. It cannot determine whether accepting that uneven, unaddressed outcome is preferable to the risks of active intervention — that is a value choice about the acceptability of inaction as policy.
content.tension: Relying on demographic decline and statutory class-size mechanics to shrink the selective track requires no legislative risk and has strong empirical grounding, but leaves every documented sorting mechanism (SES access, coaching, Roma segregation, urban/rural imbalance) untouched and may worsen geographic equity by preserving urban flagship prestige while eroding only marginal rural provision.
content.title: Passive demographic drift versus active policy intervention as the default
